text: Video demonstrating the performance of a laparoscopic duodenal switch.

The patient is a fifty-year-old gentleman with BMI of sixty-eight. The patient has had preoperative weight loss which aided him in weight loss from four hundred and forty pounds to four hundred and twenty pounds.
The patient is positioned in a lithotomy position, lying on their back with legs elevated and supported in stirrups, preparing for the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon performs a sleeve gastrectomy, selecting a point approximately six centimeters from the pylorus and mobilizing the greater curvature. A loose sleeve gastrectomy is executed over a forty French bougie, ensuring the incisura angularis is not impinged. The procedure utilizes a green load staple line reinforcement, with careful removal of the fundus.

The surgeon stands between the patient's legs and mobilizes the first and second portions of the duodenum. The pylorus is identified, and the duodenum is carefully dissected and mobilized both inferiorly and superiorly, ensuring no injury occurs. Approximately four to five centimeters of the postpyloric duodenum are mobilized.

The surgeon uses antral traction to achieve adequate mobilization of the duodenum. A plane is created just lateral to the common bile duct, and articulating sectors are used to form a retro duodenal tunnel. A pendular strain is applied to facilitate further mobilization of the duodenum.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler with reinforcement to transect the fully mobilized duodenum, elevating it with a pendros strain to facilitate division, particularly in super obese patients.

After transecting the duodenum, the surgeon moves to the left side of the patient and identifies the ileocecal junction. The bowel is rotated in an anti-clockwise direction, marking it at 125 centimeters to indicate the length of the common channel. Another 125 centimeters is measured, ensuring the bowel is correctly positioned by continuing the anti-clockwise rotation.
The surgeon divides the bowel at 250 centimeters using a white load stapler. Following this, the mesentery is carefully divided.

The surgeon marks the biliary limb with a clip, then rotates the real limb to align it with the common channel. Incisions are made, and an anastomosis is fashioned between the biliary limb and the common channel.

The surgeon creates an H-shaped anastomosis to prevent narrowing in the distant and narrow-caliber bowel. This is achieved by bidirectional firing using white load staplers, facilitating a large anastomosis. The stapler is placed in the second direction, and the common enterotomy is closed transversely to complete the H-shaped anastomosis.

The surgeon exposes the mesenteric defect by reflecting the small bowel upwards and closes it with a running 2-0 silk stitch. The surgeon then resumes position between the patient's legs and prepares to take the Roux limb in an anti-colic fashion.

The surgeon creates a retrocolic tunnel to facilitate alignment of the small bowel with the post pyloric duodenum. A stay suture is placed for alignment, followed by a posterior running suture using a two-oh vicryl stitch, meticulously forming the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a tuovacral stitch to complete the posterior row of the anastomosis between the duodenum and ileum. The harmonic scalpel is employed to open both the ileum and post pyloric duodenum, sealing the mucosa to prevent eversion. A second layer is placed to ensure a wide anastomosis, avoiding narrowing.
The surgeon uses a two ovicle stitch to perform the anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment and secure connection of the intestinal segments.

The patient is placed in lithotomy position with diamond configuration port placement and the procedure is performed with six strokers. The average length of the procedure is about three, three and a half hours. The gallbladder is not removed unless the patient has any specific gallbladder issues. The patients are put on ActiGall and managed postoperatively. The patient gets intense nutritional follow-up to facilitate and aid in weight loss.
The surgeon runs sutures from superior to inferior to tie the anastomosis, followed by the placement of a second layer anteriorly to approximate the serosa.

The surgeon examines the completed anastomosis using an endoscope or methylene blue test, ensuring its integrity. A drain is visibly placed around the anastomosis, which will be removed on post-operative day three.
